# UC-21.2.16 · Supply Chain Multi-Tier Visibility and Geospatial Supplier Risk

## Description

This use case rolls up on-time delivery rate, quality acceptance rate, and the latest geographic risk index for each supplier across supply-chain tiers and countries, then applies a weighted composite risk score and flags tier-one suppliers with many distinct parts and weak delivery performance.

## Value

Procurement and plant leadership can see concentration and fragility before a shortage stops a line, so they can qualify alternates, shift volume, or stage buffer stock while spend and customer commitments are still manageable instead of reacting after missed shipments.

## Implementation

Land normalized purchase-order and scorecard rows on `index=scm` with consistent `supplier_id` and `tier` (1 = direct strategic). Refresh supplier risk scores on a schedule that matches your third-party feed. Tune composite weights and the `composite_risk` and tier-one gates with sourcing; add a lookup for commodity family if one supplier masks many part numbers. **Domain context:** tier labels must match your bill-of-material mapping. **Splunk:** `latest()` on `geo_risk_index` assumes one risk row per bucket—collapse to `max()` if multiple events land in the window.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Splunk DB Connect (or an approved recurring export) with read-only access to purchase-order facts and payment terms, plus a supplier risk feed landed as `supplier:scorecard` or merged at index time.
• Agreed definitions for `tier` (direct vs sub-tier), `supplier_id` keys that join ERP to the risk vendor file, and currency-normalized `order_value`.
• Golden batch of ten suppliers with known geography and performance for UAT sign-off.

Create search
• Create an index-time or search-time field alias so `on_time_pct` and `quality_accept_pct` are decimals from 0–100, not strings.
• Paste the provided SPL into Search, set a 30- to 90-day window, and save as a report named `scm_supplier_composite_risk`.
• Optional: materialize nightly with `outputlookup` for executive dashboards if join volume is high.

Understanding this SPL
The search scopes `index=scm` to the two sourcetypes, then `stats` averages delivery and quality, takes the most recent geo risk, counts distinct parts, and sums spend **by supplier_id, supplier_country, tier**. The `eval` builds a weighted `composite_risk` from delivery shortfall, quality shortfall, and geo risk. The `where` clause surfaces high composite scores or aggressive tier-one concentration with many parts and delivery under 90%. Results sort by descending risk for triage.

Validate
• Reconcile `total_spend` and top part families for two suppliers against the ERP spend report for the same window.
• Confirm `geo_risk_index` matches the vendor portal or risk API for a sample country row on the same calendar day.
• Spot-check `on_time_pct` math against three PO lines with known receipt dates.

Operationalize
• Schedule the saved search daily; route high `composite_risk` or tier-one breaches to sourcing war-room mail or chat with deep links.
• Publish a dashboard with map, ranked bar, and detail table; document threshold changes in the sourcing runbook.
• When an alert fires, runbook steps: confirm data freshness, validate a demand spike did not distort averages, then trigger alternate-source workflow or Engineering change review.

## SPL

```spl
index=scm sourcetype IN ("erp:purchase_order","supplier:scorecard")
| stats avg(on_time_pct) as delivery_score, avg(quality_accept_pct) as quality_score, latest(geo_risk_index) as geo_risk, dc(part_number) as parts_supplied, sum(order_value) as total_spend by supplier_id, supplier_country, tier
| eval composite_risk=round((100-delivery_score)*0.3 + (100-quality_score)*0.3 + geo_risk*0.4, 1)
| where composite_risk > 60 OR (tier=1 AND parts_supplied > 50 AND delivery_score < 90)
| sort -composite_risk
```

## Visualization

Bubble map or choropleth (supplier_country by composite_risk), Bar chart (top suppliers by composite_risk), Table (supplier_id, tier, parts_supplied, delivery_score, quality_score, geo_risk, total_spend), Single value (count of suppliers above risk threshold).

## Known False Positives

A supplier can look risky after a one-off port strike or weather event that already cleared, or when on-time metrics mix air and ocean lanes. Single-site quality blips from a known engineering change order can depress acceptance until the new drawing stabilizes. Geo risk indexes that lag peace dividends or sanctions updates can over- or under-state exposure. Heavy spend concentration on a sole-source part is legitimate until a second source qualifies—tiers and part counts need master-data hygiene so false churn does not bury true concentration.
